data.content:
<role_definition>
You are building an auditor for biotech catalyst theses. A company claims it has cash to reach a trial readout. The cash side comes from SEC filings, the date side from a registry the sponsor controls and can revise at will, and nothing reconciles a revision against the thesis that depended on the old date. This project is that reconciliation.
The whole claim rests on every figure being traceable to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry version, so you are precise about provenance and you would rather ask than guess at an interface you have not read. You match the house style you find rather than importing habits from elsewhere.

</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. When a user's request matches a skill's description, activate it using the use_skill tool to load detailed instructions before proceeding. Only activate skills that are clearly relevant to the current task. Activate each skill once per current context window. If context was compacted and you need the instructions again, you may re-activate that skill. Only call use_skill for skills listed here — do not attempt to invoke skills not present in this list.
<available_skills>
  <skill>
    <name>create-skill</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new Bob skill (SKILL.md) — covers the frontmatter schema, name rules, file location, and gotchas. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a skill.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>create-mode</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new custom Bob mode (custom_modes.yaml) — covers the schema, permission groups, roleDefinition, and file location. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a mode or persona.</description>
  </skill>

    <description>Add a new MCP server or diagnose an existing one — covers local vs remote transport, system dependency checks (node, docker, podman, python, uv), config schema validation, secrets handling, and scope. Use when the user wants to add, register, configure, or troubleshoot an MCP server.</description>
  </skill>

    <description>Guide for building a custom MCP server from scratch — covers project scaffolding, the v2 registerTool/registerResource/registerPrompt API, API key and OAuth authentication patterns, building and registering the server. Use when the user wants to create, build, or write a custom MCP server.</description>

ALWAYS use when she names an automated/recorded/timed round — assessment, OA, online assessment, HireVue, video interview, Pymetrics, Codility, HackerRank, CodeSignal, SHL, Superday format, coding challenge, aptitude test — in ANY of these shapes — "what's the <firm> OA like", "what should I expect for the <firm> assessment", "research the <firm> HireVue", "find past questions for <firm>", "prep me for the <firm> online assessment", AND the urgent framings "help me prep for my <firm> assessment tomorrow", "I have a <firm> OA later today", "prep me for my HireVue tonight", "<firm> assessment in 2 hours". Urgency does NOT route this to meeting prep — if the round is automated, it is THIS skill regardless of how soon it is. ROUTING TEST — is there a live human on the other end? NO (recorded/timed/auto-graded) → this skill. YES (a named person, a call, a ...</description>

Don't use when: user asks for live odds or market data — use polymarket, kalshi, or the sport-specific skill to fetch odds first, then use this skill to analyze them.</description>

    <description>Drill partner and solver for Criteria Cognitive Aptitude Test (CCAT) items — the Criteria Corp aptitude format used by KKR's 2027 Summer Analyst screen (OPP-2026-429) and many other finance/consulting employers on ondemandassessment.com. Use when Kristen names CCAT, Criteria, KKR's screen, ondemandassessment.com or "cognitive aptitude", or when she is practicing against a CCAT bank.

ROUTING — owns CCAT pacing, scoring, spatial technique, odd-one-out and matrix items. For the shared trap tables on number series, syllogisms and percentage math, defer to shl-drill rather than duplicating them. Does NOT own: SHL / IMC-format assessments (shl-...</description>

    <description>Kristen's cover-letter writer. ALWAYS use this when she wants a cover letter for a job or firm — "write a cover letter for <firm>", "cover letter for the <role>", "make a cover letter", "docx cover letter", or when the fill-application flow needs one for a posting.     <description>Orientation and consistency prep for the Criteria Corp behavioral assessment — the 10-minute personality inventory (almost certainly the EPP, Employee Personality Profile) paired with the CCAT on ondemandassessment.com, used by KKR's 2027 Summer Analyst screen (OPP-2026-429) and other finance employers. <project_rules>

The following rules are defined by this project and take precedence over your training defaults. They apply to every response for the duration of this session — do not revert to your defaults as the conversation grows longer. Before taking any action, check whether a project rule applies. Where rules from different sources conflict, the more specific source takes precedence: workspace rules override global rules, and mode-specific rules override common rules.

# Project Instructions (AGENTS.md)

# AGENTS.md — for IBM Bob

`CLAUDE.md` sits beside this file and is addressed to a different tool. Where the
two disagree about who builds what, this file governs your work.

**You are the primary development tool on this project.** The build prompts in
`docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` are addressed to you. Claude verifies your output, runs the
kill gates, and writes docs; it does not execute those prompts.

Read `HANDOFF.md` first, then `docs/SPEC.md`. Read `docs/FINDINGS.md` before
touching any extraction code: it records eight failure modes found empirically,
and every one of them produces a wrong number rather than an error.

## What this is

Every biotech thesis rests on a date. The company has cash into X, the trial
reads out before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side
comes from SEC filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the
sponsor sets it, can revise it whenever it likes, and nothing reconciles that
revision against the thesis that depended on the old date.

This project treats that as a contract and audits it.

## The rule everything follows

**Python computes. Granite judges prose. Humans decide.**

No model-produced number reaches the user. `_fabricated()` in the ported
`granite.py` enforces it and is ported byte for byte:

- Do **not** tighten it to ban all digits. That rejects correct output quoting a
  figure from the belief's own claim text, which is quotation, not invention.
- Do **not** loosen it. The rule is "a number absent from the input".

## Credentials

Granite needs `WATSONX_API_KEY`, `WATSONX_PROJECT_ID` and `WATSONX_URL`. They
live in `.env` at the repo root, which is gitignored and which you cannot read,
because "Respect .gitignore" is on. You do not need to read it. You need to load
it, in the same shell command as whatever you are running:

```bash
set -a; . ./.env; set +a; python3 -m pytest tests/
```

A shell that has not sourced `.env` has no credentials, so `GraniteClassifier`
cannot authenticate and anything requiring live Granite fails. That failure is
correct behaviour, not a bug to work around: silently falling back to the stub
is how a stub judgment ends up in a demo that claims Granite made it.

Never print the values, never paste them into a message, and never write them
into a file that is not `.env`.

## Do not rewrite the engine

`engine/runway.py`, `engine/ctgov_history.py` and `engine/gap.py` predate you and
are verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. They are the reason
this project is credible. Run all three before and after any change:

```bash
python3 -m engine.runway
python3 -m engine.ctgov_history
python3 -m engine.gap
```

If a change of yours needs one of them modified, stop and say so rather than
editing it. That has been the right call every time it has come up.

## Four invariants

**Every displayed number traces to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry
version.** `Runway.provenance` exists for this. A figure that cannot name its
source does not get displayed.

**Unreliable rows are shown, never ranked, never silently dropped.** A screen
that hides its hard cases is worse than one that shows them. See
`Runway.reliable`. Arrowhead computes to a 1,116 month runway in this engine
because of a partnership upfront; the answer is to flag it, not to hide it.

**A lapsed completion date is never a catalyst.** You cannot run out of money
before an event that already happened. The binding catalyst is the nearest
registered primary completion still in the future. Lapsed dates are retained and
surfaced as date-integrity signals, which is the entire point of the project.

**Burn is a band, not a point.** One quarter containing a partnership upfront is
unrepresentative, and a single number would hide that.

## Language

Say **registered primary-completion expectation**, never "readout date". They
differ systematically by two to four months and the gap is always optimistic.

Say "we could not find", never "nobody has". A commercial screener and a
published revision dataset both exist and are cited in `docs/DEMO.md`. The claim
is the monitor, not a novel dataset and not a finding.

## Voice and commits

Comments say why, not what. No em dashes. Plain language, no filler. Commit as
kristenharim with plain messages, and never add AI attribution, `Co-Authored-By`,
or generated-with footers to any commit or file.

## Never touch

`~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` is a separate working project and the fallback
demo for this submission. Phase 2 copies four files out of it, listed in
`docs/PORT.md`. Read those. Change nothing there, ever.

## Log what you build

Add a row to `docs/BOB_LOG.md` for each task, naming the tool. The README's Bob
section is written from that file rather than from memory, so a line that says
"by hand" must never be described later as yours.

**You are IBM Bob. Write `IBM Bob` in the Tool column, and nothing else.** Do not
copy the value from the row above and do not name the model you believe you are.
This has already gone wrong twice: you wrote `Claude Code` for your own work on
the redline loop and again on the console, which understated your contribution on
the one requirement the submission cannot lose. In the Transcript column name the
export file if one exists yet, otherwise write `pending export`.

</project_rules>

Contents of file HANDOFF.md:

1 | # Handoff: start here
2 | 
3 | This repo is a de-risked starting point, not a finished project. Three engine modules
4 | are built and verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. Everything else is
5 | specified but unbuilt, and that is deliberate: the unbuilt parts are what IBM Bob should
6 | build, because the challenge requires Bob to be the primary development tool.
7 | 
8 | ## What to do with the IBM chat you already have open
9 | 
10 | Pause it. Do not redirect it mid thread.
11 | 
12 | That thread's context is saturated with the portfolio risk framing, so a correction
13 | inside it will keep drifting back to belief cards about VaR. A pivot this size wants a
14 | clean context whose first input is a written spec.
15 | 
16 | Also: do not touch `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk`. It runs, it talks to live Granite,
17 | and it is the fallback demo if this build stalls. Its value is that it exists.
18 | 
19 | **Open a new chat and paste this as the first message:**
20 | 
21 | > I am building the Catalyst Integrity Desk for the IBM AI Builders Challenge, with IBM
22 | > Bob as the primary development tool. The repo is at `~/projects/catalyst-integrity-desk`.
23 | >
24 | > Read these first, in order: `HANDOFF.md`, `docs/SPEC.md`, `docs/FINDINGS.md`,
25 | > `docs/PORT.md`. `docs/FINDINGS.md` contains eight data gotchas that were found
26 | > empirically and each one will cost you a day if you rediscover it.
27 | >
28 | > The three modules in `engine/` are verified working. Do not rewrite them. Run
29 | > `python3 -m engine.runway`, `python3 -m engine.ctgov_history`, and `python3 -m engine.gap`
30 | > to confirm before you change anything.
31 | >
32 | > Start with Phase 1 in `docs/SPEC.md`. Use `docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` for the sequenced
33 | > build prompts.
34 | 
35 | ## What is verified working, right now
36 | 
37 | Run these. They hit live APIs and assert on real data.
38 | 
39 | ```
40 | python3 -m engine.runway          # cash runway from SEC XBRL, 12 clinical-stage names
41 | python3 -m engine.ctgov_history   # registry revision history for one trial
42 | python3 -m engine.gap             # the join: funding gap + date integrity
43 | ```
44 | 
45 | Measured on 2026-07-21:
46 | 
47 | - `NCT04613596` (Mirati): 95 protocol versions, 6 of which moved the primary
48 |   completion date, net slip +2,193 days.
49 | - `NCT04248439` (Rocket Pharmaceuticals): **carried a completion date that had already
50 |   passed for 677 days** before correcting it.
51 | - 12 of 12 clinical-stage, pre-revenue names produced rankable runway bands. Sana
52 |   8.1-9.2 months, Prime Medicine 9.5-10.4, Rocket 9.5-9.6.
53 | 
54 | ## The one paragraph version of the idea
55 | 
56 | Every biotech thesis rests on a date: the company has cash into X, the trial reads out
57 | before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side comes from SEC
58 | filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the sponsor sets it, can
59 | revise it at any time, and the revision is not reconciled against the thesis that
60 | depended on the old date.
61 | 
62 | The Catalyst Integrity Desk treats that as a contract: this company has capital to reach
63 | this readout by this date. Python computes both sides from filed tags and registry
64 | records, with no model near a number. Granite reads the analyst's written rationale and
65 | reports which stated assumption a new filing or amendment breaks. The analyst approves,
66 | and the decision hash chains into a tamper evident ledger.
67 | 
68 | So the desk carries a third column: how the sponsor has behaved toward its own date.
69 | 
70 | ## How to frame it, exactly
71 | 
72 | **"An auditable monitor for when a portfolio's cash-to-catalyst assumption breaks."**
73 | 
74 | Not "a novel dataset" and not "a new finding". Both of those lose under one question:
75 | 
76 | - The catalyst screen is a shipping commercial product. BiopharmaWatch filters 11,000+
77 |   readouts by cash runway and burn across 949 companies, including a trial-change field.
78 | - The revision panel is a published dataset: 4.3M per-version rows across ~583k trials.
79 | - The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent published work (Guenzel & Liu, RFS 2026).
80 | 
81 | See `docs/FINDINGS.md` section 2. What survives is the *monitor*: a contract that is
82 | recomputed deterministically, breached visibly, judged against its own written rationale,
83 | and changed only by a human whose decision is hash chained. That is a real thing to build
84 | and an honest thing to claim.
85 | 
86 | ## What must not be claimed
87 | 
88 | That cash constrained sponsors revise their readout dates differently than solvent ones.
89 | **Hypothesis, untested, and adjacent work already exists.** Say "we could not find" and
90 | never "nobody has".
91 | 
92 | State the identification problem out loud: low runway correlates with small, under
93 | resourced companies that slip more anyway, which pushes the opposite way from the
94 | strategic story. A judge who hears an honest "here is the panel, here is why the causal
95 | claim is hard" scores it above a confident result that dies to one question.
96 | 
97 | Also: say **registered primary-completion expectation**, not readout date. They are not
98 | the same thing and the difference is a systematic 2 to 4 month optimistic bias.
99 |

Contents of file docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md:

1 | # Bob prompt pack
2 | 
3 | Sequenced. Run them in order, one per working session, and check the acceptance criteria
4 | before moving on. Each prompt is written to be pasted whole.
5 | 
6 | Why this shape: the challenge requires IBM Bob as the primary development tool and a
7 | README section describing that use. So Bob must do genuinely substantial work, and the
8 | work must be describable honestly. These prompts hand Bob bounded, verifiable builds
9 | rather than "improve the codebase", because a bounded build produces a reviewable diff
10 | and a vague one produces churn.
11 | 
12 | **Export the session transcripts.** They are the evidence for the README section.
13 | 
14 | ---
15 | 
16 | ## Prompt 1: port the governance layer
17 | 
18 | > Read `docs/PORT.md` in this repo, then copy the four listed modules from
19 | > `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` into this project, preserving their structure.
20 | >
21 | > Do not modify `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` in any way. It is a working fallback.
22 | >
23 | > Change only what `docs/PORT.md` says to change: comments in `ledger.py`, and the two
24 | > prompt strings in `granite.py`. Keep `_fabricated()` byte for byte, including its
25 | > rule that fabrication means a number absent from the input rather than any digit at
26 | > all.
27 | >
28 | > Then write `engine/contract.py` that turns a `CatalystContract` from `engine/gap.py`
29 | > into the flat `{metric_id: value}` dict that `scan_breaches` consumes. Include a
30 | > `demo()` with asserts, matching the style of the three existing engine modules.
31 | 
32 | **Accept when:** all four original demos still pass, `python3 -m engine.contract` passes,
33 | and a contract packet round trips through `scan_breaches` producing a breach on a
34 | deliberately out of range gap.
35 | 
36 | ---
37 | 
38 | ## Prompt 2: the redline loop
39 | 
40 | > Build `orchestrator/redline.py`. Given a `CatalystContract` recomputed after a change,
41 | > and the previously approved version of the same contract, produce a `ChallengeCard`
42 | > using the ported `build_challenge`.
43 | >
44 | > Critical constraint: Granite receives the contract's rationale text and a description
45 | > of what moved **in directions, not values** ("the funding gap falls sharply", never
46 | > "the gap fell 9.2 months"). The application renders every figure. Read
47 | > `as_directions()` in `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk/engine/scenario.py` for the
48 | > pattern, then implement the equivalent here.
49 | >
50 | > Write a test that feeds Granite a breach and asserts no figure appears in its output
51 | > that was not in its input.
52 | 
53 | **Accept when:** the fabrication test passes against live Granite, and a scripted
54 | amendment produces a challenge card with a classification and a drafted memo.
55 | 
56 | ---
57 | 
58 | ## Prompt 3: the console
59 | 
60 | > Build a web console over the existing engine. Three views only:
61 | >
62 | > 1. Contract list, ranked by funding gap. Flagged rows visible but visually separated
63 | >    and unranked, with the flag reason shown.
64 | > 2. Contract detail: the gap calculation with every input labeled by its source tag or
65 | >    registry version, plus the trial's date revision timeline as a horizontal chart.
66 | >    Mark any revision where the sponsor carried an already expired date.
67 | > 3. Pending redline: the challenge card, Granite's classification and memo, and
68 | >    accept / edit / reject controls.
69 | >
70 | > Server side rendering with a small framework, no build step if avoidable. Read from a
71 | > local JSON snapshot, never a live API call during rendering. Every number displayed
72 | > must come from the engine, never recomputed in the view layer.
73 | 
74 | **Accept when:** all three views render from a frozen snapshot with the network
75 | disabled, and the ledger tamper demo is visible in the UI.
76 | 
77 | ### Prompt 3, continuation: sub-tasks 2 to 6
78 | 
79 | Sub-task 1 is built and verified. This is the rest of Prompt 3, and it is the last
80 | substantial Bob spend on this project, so it is one conversation and not five.
81 | 
82 | > Read `docs/plans/console.md`, then implement the Sub-task 1 addendum and sub-tasks
83 | > 2 through 6 in that order. Sub-task 1 itself is done: `data/snapshot.json` exists,
84 | > was built against live Granite, and must not be regenerated. The addendum adds
85 | > display strings to it without any network call.
86 | >
87 | > Four things decide whether this is right:
88 | >
89 | > Do the addendum first. Sub-task 6's number-provenance test cannot pass until the
90 | > rounded strings a reader sees are in the snapshot, and discovering that at sub-task
91 | > 6 costs a rewrite of every template.
92 | >
93 | > No template formats a number. Every figure prints a `display.*` string. If you
94 | > reach for `round()` or a Jinja number filter in a template, the snapshot is missing
95 | > a field and that is where the fix goes.
96 | >
97 | > `/` opens on the Rocket revision timeline and the 677-day carried-expired node is
98 | > visible without scrolling and marked distinctly from ordinary revisions. If a
99 | > layout choice would bury it, the layout loses.
100 | >
101 | > Sub-task 6 step 8 is not optional and not a formality: hardcode a `9999` into the
102 | > detail template, watch the provenance test fail and name that token, then remove
103 | > it. Three checks on this project have looked correct and been hollow. A check
104 | > nobody has seen fail is not evidence.
105 | >
106 | > Do not regenerate `data/snapshot.json`, do not call watsonx, and do not edit
107 | > anything in `engine/`.
108 | 
109 | **Accept when:** `pytest tests/test_console.py` passes, the provenance test has been
110 | seen failing on a planted token, `make_snapshot.py --displays` is idempotent, and
111 | `/` lands on the timeline with the 677 row above the fold.
112 | 
113 | ### Prompt 3, repair: the integrity badge
114 | 
115 | Verification of sub-tasks 2 to 6 found one defect and three smaller ones. The defect
116 | is the tamper demo itself. Nothing in this task calls watsonx, so the rate-limit
117 | episode that ate the last budget cannot repeat here.
118 | 
119 | Items are in priority order. If anything goes wrong, stopping after item 2 still
120 | leaves the demo intact.
121 | 
122 | > Read the verification section of `docs/STATUS.md` first.
123 | >
124 | > **1. The integrity badge is decorative.** `GET /redline/confirm` reads `intact`
125 | > from the query string, set once at decision time by the POST handler. Tamper the
126 | > ledger and reload, which is exactly what the page instructs, and the badge still
127 | > reads intact. Measured: `BeliefLedger.verify()` returns `False` on disk while the
128 | > page returns `✓ intact`. The confirm handler must open the ledger and call
129 | > `verify()` itself, at render time.
130 | >
131 | > Write the test before the fix and watch it fail. It must POST a decision, tamper a
132 | > byte the hash actually covers, and assert the badge flips. Note that the verdict
133 | > word lives in `data/review_log.jsonl`, not the ledger, so editing `approve` changes
134 | > nothing hashed and `verify()` stays `True`. That was a real false negative during
135 | > verification. Change something inside the `card` payload instead.
136 | >
137 | > **2. The app cannot bind its port.** `app.run(port=5000)` collides with macOS
138 | > ControlCenter, so `python3 console/app.py` fails to start on the demo machine. Read
139 | > the port from the `PORT` environment variable, defaulting to something free.
140 | >

Contents of file docs/FINDINGS.md:

1 | # Findings: data gotchas, prior art, attack surface
2 | 
3 | Everything in section 1 was found by running code against the live APIs on 2026-07-21,
4 | not read in documentation. Each one silently produces a wrong number rather than an
5 | error, which is the expensive kind.
6 | 
7 | ## 1. Data gotchas, each with the fix
8 | 
9 | ### 1.1 The registry history endpoint is TLS fingerprint gated
10 | 
11 | `/api/int/studies/{nct}/history` returns 403 to `urllib` and will do the same to
12 | `requests`, because both share OpenSSL's TLS fingerprint. It returns 200 to `curl`
13 | with any User-Agent, including a bare one.
14 | 
15 | Tested four header combinations (bare UA, curl-style UA, browser UA, browser UA plus
16 | gzip and Accept) against a curl control. All four urllib variants 403, curl 200. It is
17 | not the headers.
18 | 
19 | **Fix:** shell out to curl, which ships with macOS and needs no dependency. See
20 | `engine/ctgov_history.py::_get`. `curl_cffi` would also work and adds a dependency.
21 | 
22 | ### 1.2 A cash tag can go stale while the company is fine
23 | 
24 | Arrowhead stopped filing `CashAndCashEquivalentsAtCarryingValue` in 2024 and moved to
25 | `CashCashEquivalentsRestrictedCashAndRestrictedCashEquivalents`. A first-wins tag
26 | waterfall returned the last value of the abandoned tag: **$69M as of 2024-06-30, when
27 | the real figure was $3,377M as of 2026-03-31.** A 49x error, and it dated the entire
28 | record to 2024, which then broke the securities match too.
29 | 
30 | This does not look like a bug. It looks like a company about to die, which is exactly
31 | the row a distress screen puts at the top.
32 | 
33 | **Fix:** evaluate every cash tag and take the one with the most recent balance date,
34 | not the first that exists. Net out restricted cash when the winning tag bundles it.
35 | 
36 | ### 1.3 Cash flow facts are year-to-date, so "quarterly" filtering lies
37 | 
38 | Cash flow statements are cumulative within a fiscal year. Q1 is natively a 90 day fact,
39 | Q2 arrives as a 6 month figure, Q3 as 9 month, Q4 inside the 10-K as 12 month. So
40 | filtering XBRL duration facts to a 60-120 day span does not return the last four
41 | quarters. **It returns Q1 of four consecutive years**, and summing those produces a
42 | plausible looking annual burn that is the trailing twelve months of nothing.
43 | 
44 | Observed directly: Moderna's "quarterly" facts came back as 2026-03-31, 2025-03-31,
45 | 2024-03-31, 2023-03-31.
46 | 
47 | **Fix:** facts within one fiscal year share a `start`, so group on `start`, sort by
48 | `end`, and take consecutive differences. See `engine/runway.py::_quarterly_flows`.
49 | 
50 | ### 1.4 Short term investments have no single tag, and it matters most where it matters
51 | 
52 | There is no `CashCashEquivalentsAndShortTermInvestments` tag. It is never filed. Real
53 | coverage across clinical-stage filers is roughly 45%, split across at least three tags:
54 | `ShortTermInvestments`, `MarketableSecuritiesCurrent`,
55 | `AvailableForSaleSecuritiesDebtSecuritiesCurrent`. All four appear in the 12 name test
56 | set, and Ionis uses two at once.
57 | 
58 | Part of the 45% is genuine (small companies hold only cash) and part is fragmentation.
59 | Either way the missing half skews toward the well capitalized companies whose runway is
60 | most interesting.
61 | 
62 | **Fix:** sum across all securities tags, but only those struck on the same date as the
63 | cash balance, or the numerator mixes two quarters. Record which path resolved.
64 | 
65 | ### 1.5 `company_tickers.json` is missing live filers
66 | 
67 | The file is complete (10,426 entries, 870 KB) and Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD) is simply
68 | not in it. Neither is Verve.
69 | 
70 | **Fix:** the ticker map is a demo convenience only. The universe comes from the DERA
71 | Financial Statement Data Sets quarterly ZIP, keyed on CIK and SIC code, where no ticker
72 | is involved. Use SIC 2834, 2835, 2836. SIC 8731 is a red herring: 15 filings against
73 | 412 at 2834.
74 | 
75 | ### 1.6 Operating cash flow is not burn for a company with revenue
76 | 
77 | Arrowhead's trailing year contains an $825M partnership inflow, netting its burn to near
78 | zero and computing a **1,116 month runway**. Arithmetically correct, financially
79 | meaningless.
80 | 
81 | **Fix:** flag any cash-positive operating quarter in the trailing window and any case
82 | where the two burn estimates disagree by more than 3x. Flagged rows stay visible with
83 | the reason attached but never carry a rank. On the correct population (clinical-stage,
84 | pre-revenue) the flag rate went from 5 of 8 commercial names to 0 of 12.
85 | 
86 | ### 1.7 Sponsors revise dates backwards, and it is a typo
87 | 
88 | `NCT04613596` moved its completion date +1,317 days at version 92 and then -1,317 days
89 | at version 94, two months later. That is data entry, not a forecast revision. Any
90 | statistic over revision magnitudes needs a reversal filter.
91 | 
92 | ### 1.8 The sponsor to CIK join is the real engineering tax
93 | 
94 | `leadSponsor.name` is free text with no CIK. Forward matching (EDGAR name to registry)
95 | runs about 83% naively, and most misses are correct rejections (animal health, cannabis,
96 | shell companies). Sana and Intellia both failed to match in the demo. Realistic ceiling
97 | with an alias table plus hand review of the top 300 by market cap is 90-95%.
98 | 
99 | Budget a full day for this. It is the single largest unbudgeted cost in the project.
100 | 
101 | ### 1.9 `query.spons` matches collaborators, so a partner's trial becomes your catalyst
102 | 
103 | The v2 search parameter `query.spons` is not a lead-sponsor filter. It matches the
104 | sponsor/collaborator block as a whole, so a trial another company runs comes back under
105 | your issuer's name and `engine/gap.py` assigns it as that issuer's binding catalyst.
106 | 
107 | Observed: `find_trials("ARVINAS, INC.")` returns `NCT05654623` first. Its `leadSponsor`
108 | is **Pfizer**; Arvinas Estrogen Receptor, Inc. is a collaborator. Arvinas's funding gap
109 | was therefore computed against a Pfizer-run trial.
110 | 
111 | One of 8 joined names in the 12 name set. It does not error and it does not look wrong on
112 | screen, because the trial is real and the drug is genuinely theirs.
113 | 
114 | **Fix:** after searching, keep only trials whose `leadSponsor.name` matches the issuer,
115 | normalising the suffix noise (`Inc.`/`Inc`/`, Inc`/`AG`/`Corp`). The registry's lead
116 | sponsor string differs from EDGAR's registrant name in 3 of 8 joins even when correct
117 | (`Rocket Pharmaceuticals Inc.` vs `ROCKET PHARMACEUTICALS, INC.`), so match normalised,
118 | not literal.
119 | 
120 | ### 1.10 The nearest registered completion is usually the most stale one
121 | 
122 | `find_trials` sorts ascending by primary completion date and `build` takes `trials[0]`,
123 | which is the *earliest* date, not the *next* one. Because sponsors carry lapsed dates
124 | (finding 1.7 and the Rocket case), the earliest date is frequently already in the past,
125 | so the selection rule is biased toward exactly the stalest record in the set.
126 | 
127 | Three of 8 joined names picked an already-passed completion date on 2026-07-21: ARVN
128 | (2025-01-31), EDIT (2025-08), RCKT (2026-05-05).
129 | 
130 | This one is not cosmetic. Rocket is the demo's hero row. Against its picked date the gap
131 | is **+8.4 months, "funded to catalyst"**. Against its nearest *future* registered
132 | completion (`NCT06092034`, 2028-04) the same runway gives **-14.5 months, "financing
133 | required"**. Same company, same filing, opposite verdict, decided entirely by which
134 | registered date is treated as binding.
135 | 
136 | **Fix is a definition, not a patch, and it is Kristen's call:** decide whether the binding
137 | catalyst is the nearest completion date still in the future, or the nearest one whose
138 | trial is still live regardless of a lapsed date. The second is defensible and is arguably
139 | the project's whole point, but then the gap must be presented against a date the sponsor
140 | has already missed, and the demo has to say so out loud rather than print
141 | "funded to catalyst".
142 | 
143 | ## 2. Prior art, stated honestly
144 | 
145 | **The screen is not novel.** [BiopharmaWatch Catalyst Sync](https://www.biopharmawatch.com/catalyst-sync)
146 | sells exactly it: filter 11,000+ upcoming readouts by cash runway and burn rate across
147 | 949 companies. BioPharmCatalyst, Biomedtracker/Citeline, and Stifel's weekly biopharma
148 | update all occupy adjacent ground. EY Beyond Borders publishes the aggregate annually
149 | (33% of public biotechs under one year of runway at end 2025).
150 | 
151 | Do not pitch the screen. A judge finds BiopharmaWatch in one search.
152 | 
153 | **The revision panel is also not novel, and this was an overclaim in an earlier draft of
154 | this document.** [brbk/clinical_trials_history](https://huggingface.co/datasets/brbk/clinical_trials_history)
155 | is 4,333,631 rows across roughly 583,000 trials, every version, with
156 | `primary_completion_date` as a per-version field, built off the same internal endpoint.
157 | Do not say "nobody has assembled this."
158 | 
159 | Operationally this is good news: it removes the single largest engineering cost in the
160 | project. Crawling histories directly is roughly 50 to 100 requests per trial. Use the
161 | dataset for the panel and keep `engine/ctgov_history.py` for live single-trial checks in
162 | the demo, where fetching from the source is the point. Note the licence is CC-BY-NC-4.0,
163 | which covers a hackathon but not a commercial product.
164 | 
165 | **The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent, not empty.**
166 | [Guenzel & Liu, Excess Commitment in R&D, RFS 39(7) 2026](https://doi.org/10.1093/rfs/hhag026)
167 | uses clinical trial project data and finds that delays reduce subsequent project
168 | termination, instrumented with trial site congestion, moderated by CEO stock-price
169 | sensitivity. That is the opposite arrow from this project's hypothesis (delay causing
170 | firm behaviour, versus firm finances causing date revision behaviour), but it means the
171 | territory is occupied and "no paper connects finance and trial timing" is indefensible.
172 | Cite it, distinguish the arrow, and never claim empty terrain.
173 | 
174 | **What still appears open,** stated as a search result rather than a fact: we could not
175 | find work on sponsor liquidity predicting *disclosure* behaviour toward the registered
176 | date specifically, meaning how long a lapsed date is carried and how much notice a
177 | revision gives. That is narrower than "the intersection is unoccupied" and it is the
178 | most that can be honestly claimed without a systematic review.
179 | 
180 | Background that does exist: the trial delay literature
181 | ([Shadbolt et al., JAMA Netw Open 2023](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2800488),
182 | about 1 in 5 RCTs complete on time, median delay 12.2 months), the primary outcome
183 | switching literature, and the biotech financing literature
184 | ([Lerner, Shane & Tsai, JFE 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304405X02002568)).
185 | 
186 | ## 3. The five hardest questions
187 | 
188 | **"Your burn number is wrong for exactly the companies that matter."**
189 | Correct. A Phase 3 ramp burns above the trailing rate; a partnership upfront shows a
190 | positive quarter. That is why burn is a band and unstable rows are excluded from
191 | ranking rather than silently included. Publish the sensitivity.
192 | 
193 | **"Primary completion date is not a readout."**
194 | Correct, and it is a systematic bias, not noise. PCD is last patient last visit for the
195 | primary endpoint; topline follows by weeks to months. The gap is optimistic by roughly
196 | 2 to 4 months on every row. The honest reframe is that the number is the sponsor's own
197 | registered expectation, which is weaker as a product and stronger as research, because
198 | the sponsor's stated belief is precisely the object of study.
199 | 
200 | **"Companies raise opportunistically, not at zero cash."**
201 | Concede fully. This is why "negative gap predicts financing" is close to tautological: a
202 | company with six months of cash raises within six months regardless. It also strengthens
203 | the real hypothesis, because if raising is window dependent then a company facing a
204 | closed window has a stronger reason to manage the visible date.
205 | 
206 | **"Survivorship."**
207 | Real. The public biotech universe shrank from 977 to 758 companies between 2021 and
208 | 2025, and the missing names are non randomly the negative gap tail. DERA is available
209 | per quarter back to 2009, so rebuild the universe as of each historical quarter. One
210 | afternoon, and it is the difference between a snapshot and a panel.
211 | 
212 | **"The interesting companies are the ones your arithmetic fails on."**
213 | Royalty monetization, non dilutive upfronts, debt facilities with milestone tranches,
214 | priority review voucher sales. Every one breaks cash divided by burn. This is the best
215 | justification for the model in the architecture: not to extract numbers, but to read the
216 | going concern and subsequent events footnotes and emit a categorical flag. Company
217 | guided runway versus computed runway is itself a chart worth showing.
218 |

109 | class _TextCollector(HTMLParser):
110 |     """Walk all text nodes (not attribute values) and collect visible text."""
111 | 
112 |     def __init__(self):
113 |         super().__init__()
114 |         self._skip = False
115 |         self.chunks: list[str] = []
116 | 
117 |     def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs):
118 |         # style and script content is not user-visible text.
119 |         if tag in ("style", "script"):
120 |             self._skip = True
121 | 
122 |     def handle_endtag(self, tag):
123 |         if tag in ("style", "script"):
124 |             self._skip = False
125 | 
126 |     def handle_data(self, data):
127 |         if not self._skip:
128 |             self.chunks.append(data)
129 | 
130 | 
131 | _TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")
132 | 
133 | 
134 | def _visible_numbers(html: bytes) -> list[str]:
135 |     """Extract every number-like token from the visible text of an HTML page."""
136 |     parser = _TextCollector()
137 |     parser.feed(html.decode())
138 |     text = " ".join(parser.chunks)
139 |     return _TOKEN_RE.findall(text)
140 |

145 |

147 |     computed in the view layer' invariant.  Attribute values (viewBox, r,
148 |     stroke-width, etc.) are not text nodes and are not reached by the parser.
149 |     SVG x positions are stored in the snapshot (sub-task 1) and pass on their
150 |     own merit.
151 | 
152 |     Failure verification (performed before finalising — see module docstring):
153 |     a literal '9999' was temporarily added to a <td> in detail.html and this
154 |     test correctly failed, naming '9999' as the offending token on
155 |     GET /contract/RCKT.
156 |     """
157 |     r = client.get(route)
158 |     assert r.status_code == 200
159 |     tokens = _visible_numbers(r.data)
160 |     for token in tokens:
161 |         assert token in snapshot_raw, (
162 |             f"number token {token!r} from GET {route} not found in snapshot.json\n"
163 |             f"(This means a number was computed or hardcoded in the view layer.)"
164 |         )

1 | """Build data/snapshot.json for the console.
2 | 
3 | Captures the full engine output for the demo tickers (SANA, PRME, RCKT) plus one
4 | scripted amendment for RCKT so the Flask app never touches a live API during rendering.

11 |     python3 console/make_snapshot.py --verify
12 | """

14 |

21 | from dataclasses import asdict

30 | from orchestrator.granite import GraniteClassifier

36 | SVG_PAD = 60  # pixels each side; usable span = SVG_WIDTH - 2*SVG_PAD = 980
37 | 
38 | # The scripted amendment shifts RCKT's catalyst_date forward by this many months
39 | # so gap_months goes negative and a breach fires.

47 | def _fmt_m(v: float) -> str:
48 |     """Format dollars as '$NNNm' with zero decimal places (e.g. '$182M').
49 |     Stored in the snapshot so the template can output the string verbatim
50 |     and the provenance test finds it as a substring of the snapshot.

53 |

66 |     so the provenance test can find them verbatim in the snapshot.  Rounding
67 |     happens here, not in the template.

70 |         "ticker": r.ticker,

72 |         "name": r.name,
73 |         "as_of": r.as_of,
74 |         "cash": r.cash,

76 |         "liquidity": r.liquidity,

78 |         "burn_recent_annual": r.burn_recent_annual,
79 |         "months_low": r.months_low,
80 |         "months_high": r.months_high,
81 |         "reliable": r.reliable,
82 |         "burn_unstable": r.burn_unstable,
83 |         "inflow_quarters": r.inflow_quarters,

100 | def _iso(d: date | None) -> str | None:
101 |     return d.isoformat() if d is not None else None
102 | 
103 | 
104 | def _add_svg_x(revisions: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
105 |     """Compute and attach svg_x to each revision dict (mutates in place, returns list).
106 | 
107 |     Maps each revision's submitted date linearly onto a 1100px canvas with 60px
108 |     padding each side.  When there is only one revision the single node sits centred.
109 |     """
110 |     dates = []
111 |     for r in revisions:
112 |         try:
113 |             dates.append(date.fromisoformat(r["submitted"]))
114 |         except (ValueError, TypeError):
115 |             dates.append(None)
116 | 
117 |     real_dates = [d for d in dates if d is not None]
118 |     if not real_dates:
119 |         for r in revisions:
120 |             r["svg_x"] = SVG_PAD + (SVG_WIDTH - 2 * SVG_PAD) / 2
121 |         return revisions
122 | 
123 |     span_start = min(real_dates)
124 |     span_end = max(real_dates)
125 |     total_days = (span_end - span_start).days or 1  # avoid div-by-zero for single node
126 | 
127 |     usable = SVG_WIDTH - 2 * SVG_PAD
128 |     for r, d in zip(revisions, dates):
129 |         if d is None:
130 |             r["svg_x"] = round(SVG_PAD + usable / 2, 1)
131 |         else:
132 |             frac = (d - span_start).days / total_days
133 |             r["svg_x"] = round(SVG_PAD + frac * usable, 1)
134 | 
135 |     return revisions
136 |

138 | def _serialise_history(history) -> dict | None:
139 |     if history is None:
140 |         return None
141 |     d = history.as_dict()
142 |     # as_dict() uses dataclasses.asdict() for revisions, which serializes fields only.
143 |     # carried_expired, days_expired and is_late_move are @property on Revision, so they
144 |     # are silently dropped.  Patch them in by reading the properties off the live objects
145 |     # rather than recomputing from held_days, so the snapshot cannot drift from the engine.
146 |     for rev_dict, rev_obj in zip(d["revisions"], history.revisions):
147 |         rev_dict["carried_expired"] = rev_obj.carried_expired
148 |         rev_dict["days_expired"] = rev_obj.days_expired
149 |         rev_dict["is_late_move"] = rev_obj.is_late_move
150 |     _add_svg_x(d["revisions"])
151 |     return d

173 | _GRANITE_BACKOFF_START = 30  # seconds; doubles each attempt

175 | 
176 | def _classify_once(delta, card) -> "ChallengeCard | None":  # type: ignore[name-defined]
177 |     """One attempt at Granite classification via run_redline.
178 | 
179 |     Returns None if the engine detected no breach — that is a broken premise,
180 |     not a case to work around.  Returns the ChallengeCard otherwise; caller
181 |     checks source.  A fresh GraniteClassifier is constructed each time so any
182 |     cached token state from a failed attempt does not carry over.
183 |     """
184 |     classifier = GraniteClassifier()
185 |     return run_redline(delta, card, classifier=classifier)
186 | 
187 | 
188 | def _build_rckt_redline(rckt: CatalystContract) -> dict:
189 |     """Construct a scripted +9-month shift on RCKT's catalyst_date and run the
190 |     redline loop with up to five attempts.  Exits non-zero if all fail."""
191 |     # Build the recomputed contract: same runway, same trial but with pcd shifted
192 |     # forward by RCKT_SHIFT_MONTHS months (approx 30.44 days/month).
193 |     original_pcd_str = rckt.trial["pcd"]
194 |     try:
195 |         original_date = date.fromisoformat(original_pcd_str)
196 |     except ValueError:
197 |         # Month-only format like "2026-05": pad to first of month.
198 |         original_date = date.fromisoformat(original_pcd_str + "-01")
199 | 
200 |     shift_days = int(RCKT_SHIFT_MONTHS * 30.4375)
201 |     new_date = original_date + timedelta(days=shift_days)
202 |     new_pcd_str = new_date.isoformat()
203 | 
204 |     new_trial = deepcopy(rckt.trial)
205 |     new_trial["pcd"] = new_pcd_str
206 | 
207 |     recomputed = CatalystContract(
208 |         runway=rckt.runway,
209 |         trial=new_trial,
210 |         history=rckt.history,
211 |     )
212 | 
213 |     delta = ContractDelta(approved=rckt, recomputed=recomputed)
214 | 
215 |     # BeliefCard: approved range requires gap_months >= 0 (funded to catalyst).
216 |     # expected_low=0 ensures the +9-month shift into negative territory triggers a breach.
217 |     original_gap = rckt.gap_months or 0.0
218 |     card = BeliefCard(
219 |         card_id="rckt:funded_to_catalyst",
220 |         scope="company:RCKT",
221 |         claim=(
222 |             "Rocket Pharmaceuticals reaches its registered primary completion date "
223 |             "before runway exhaustion, with a non-negative funding gap."
224 |         ),
225 |         metric="gap_months",
226 |         expected_low=0.0,
227 |         expected_high=max(original_gap + 2.0, 2.0),
228 |         driver="SEC XBRL liquidity (Q1-2026 10-Q) vs ClinicalTrials.gov registered PCD",
229 |         confidence=3,
230 |         source="console.make_snapshot",
231 |         as_of=rckt.runway.as_of,
232 |     )
233 | 
234 |     challenge_card = None
235 |     for attempt in range(1, _GRANITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS + 1):
236 |         print(f"  Granite attempt {attempt}/{_GRANITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS}...", flush=True)
237 |         challenge_card = _classify_once(delta, card)
238 |         if challenge_card is None:
239 |             # run_redline found no breach: the scripted amendment did not break the

Contents of file engine/ledger.py:

1 | """Append-only, hash-chained BeliefCard ledger — the governed record of what
2 | the engine *believes* about a catalyst contract.
3 | 
4 | A BeliefCard is one claim: "RCKT is funded to its primary completion date with
5 | a gap of at least zero months." The engine measures the metric; the card holds
6 | the *expected range plus the human rationale*. The Challenge step (later,
7 | Granite) reads that rationale to judge whether a breach actually matters — the
8 | card is what keeps the AI's job semantic, not a threshold check.
9 | 
10 | The ledger is event-sourced: every CREATE / UPDATE / RETIRE is appended, never
11 | mutated, and hash-chained so tampering is detectable. Only an explicit human
12 | approval appends an UPDATE — that is the whole governance guarantee.
13 | 
14 | Store: one JSONL file, one event per line. Human-readable, `cat`-able in a demo,
15 | dependency-free (stdlib). ponytail: swap to sqlite3 only if querying history at
16 | scale becomes the bottleneck — the append/verify logic stays identical.
17 | """
18 | from __future__ import annotations
19 | 
20 | import hashlib
21 | import json
22 | import os
23 | import time
24 | from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict, replace
25 | 
26 | GENESIS_HASH = "0" * 64
27 | CREATE, UPDATE, RETIRE = "CREATE", "UPDATE", "RETIRE"
28 | 
29 | 
30 | @dataclass
31 | class BeliefCard:
32 |     card_id: str
33 |     scope: str            # "company:RCKT" or "trial:NCT04248439"
34 |     claim: str            # the belief in words — the rationale Granite must read
35 |     metric: str           # engine metric_id, e.g. "gap_months"
36 |     expected_low: float
37 |     expected_high: float
38 |     driver: str
39 |     confidence: int       # 1 (weak) .. 5 (high)
40 |     source: str
41 |     as_of: str
42 |     status: str = "active"   # active | under_review | retired
43 |     version: int = 1
44 | 
45 |     def in_range(self, value: float) -> bool:
46 |         return self.expected_low <= value <= self.expected_high
47 | 
48 | 
49 | @dataclass
50 | class Breach:
51 |     card_id: str
52 |     metric: str
53 |     observed: float
54 |     expected_low: float
55 |     expected_high: float
56 |     direction: str        # "over" | "under"
57 | 
58 | 
59 | def _canonical(payload: dict) -> str:
60 |     return json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, separators=(",", ":"), default=str)
61 | 
62 | 
63 | def _hash(prev_hash: str, payload: dict) -> str:
64 |     return hashlib.sha256((prev_hash + _canonical(payload)).encode()).hexdigest()
65 | 
66 | 
67 | class BeliefLedger:
68 |     def __init__(self, path: str):
69 |         self.path = path
70 | 
71 |     # --- low-level read ---
72 |     def _entries(self) -> list[dict]:
73 |         if not os.path.exists(self.path):
74 |             return []
75 |         with open(self.path) as f:
76 |             return [json.loads(line) for line in f if line.strip()]
77 | 
78 |     def _last(self) -> dict | None:
79 |         entries = self._entries()
80 |         return entries[-1] if entries else None
81 | 
82 |     # --- append (the only writer) ---
83 |     def _append(self, event: str, card: BeliefCard, author: str,
84 |                 triggered_by: str | None, reason: str | None, ts: float | None) -> dict:
85 |         last = self._last()
86 |         prev_hash = last["entry_hash"] if last else GENESIS_HASH
87 |         seq = (last["seq"] + 1) if last else 0
88 |         payload = {
89 |             "seq": seq,
90 |             "ts": ts if ts is not None else time.time(),
91 |             "event": event,
92 |             "author": author,
93 |             "triggered_by": triggered_by,
94 |             "reason": reason,
95 |             "card": asdict(card),
96 |             "prev_hash": prev_hash,
97 |         }
98 |         entry = {**payload, "entry_hash": _hash(prev_hash, payload)}
99 |         os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(self.path) or ".", exist_ok=True)
100 |         with open(self.path, "a") as f:
101 |             f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
102 |         return entry
103 | 
104 |     def create(self, card: BeliefCard, author: str = "seed", ts: float | None = None) -> dict:
105 |         if card.card_id in self.current():
106 |             raise ValueError(f"card {card.card_id} already exists")
107 |         return self._append(CREATE, replace(card, version=1, status="active"),
108 |                             author, None, None, ts)
109 | 
110 |     def update(self, card: BeliefCard, author: str, triggered_by: str | None,
111 |                reason: str, ts: float | None = None) -> dict:
112 |         """Append an approved change. Bumps version off the current card.
113 |         `author` should be a human (e.g. "human:kristen") — this is the gate."""
114 |         cur = self.current().get(card.card_id)
115 |         if cur is None:
116 |             raise ValueError(f"card {card.card_id} does not exist")
117 |         bumped = replace(card, version=cur.version + 1)
118 |         return self._append(UPDATE, bumped, author, triggered_by, reason, ts)
119 | 
120 |     def retire(self, card_id: str, author: str, reason: str, ts: float | None = None) -> dict:
121 |         cur = self.current().get(card_id)
122 |         if cur is None:
123 |             raise ValueError(f"card {card_id} does not exist")
124 |         return self._append(RETIRE, replace(cur, status="retired", version=cur.version + 1),
125 |                             author, None, reason, ts)
126 | 
127 |     # --- read models (fold events) ---
128 |     def current(self) -> dict[str, BeliefCard]:
129 |         """Latest card state per id, excluding retired ones."""
130 |         state: dict[str, BeliefCard] = {}
131 |         for e in self._entries():
132 |             card = BeliefCard(**e["card"])
133 |             if card.status == "retired":
134 |                 state.pop(card.card_id, None)
135 |             else:
136 |                 state[card.card_id] = card
137 |         return state
138 | 
139 |     def history(self, card_id: str) -> list[dict]:
140 |         return [e for e in self._entries() if e["card"]["card_id"] == card_id]
141 | 
142 |     # --- integrity ---
143 |     def verify(self) -> bool:
144 |         """Recompute the whole chain; True iff untampered. Never raises.
145 | 
146 |         Editing a byte of the file usually breaks the JSON or drops a key rather
147 |         than producing a well-formed entry with a wrong hash, so an integrity
148 |         check that only handles the tidy case raises on the most likely kind of
149 |         tampering. A ledger that cannot be parsed is a ledger that cannot be
150 |         verified, and the honest answer to "is this intact" is no, not a
151 |         traceback. Callers render this as a status; a status that throws is worse
152 |         than one that reports false.
153 |         """
154 |         try:
155 |             entries = self._entries()
156 |         except Exception:                                       # noqa: BLE001
157 |             return False
158 | 
159 |         prev_hash = GENESIS_HASH
160 |         for i, e in enumerate(entries):
161 |             try:
162 |                 if e["seq"] != i or e["prev_hash"] != prev_hash:
163 |                     return False
164 |                 payload = {k: e[k] for k in
165 |                            ("seq", "ts", "event", "author", "triggered_by", "reason", "card", "prev_hash")}
166 |                 if _hash(prev_hash, payload) != e["entry_hash"]:
167 |                     return False
168 |                 prev_hash = e["entry_hash"]
169 |             except Exception:                                   # noqa: BLE001
170 |                 return False
171 |         return True
172 | 
173 | 
174 | def scan_breaches(cards: dict[str, BeliefCard], packet_flat: dict[str, float]) -> list[Breach]:
175 |     """Deterministic breach trigger: which active cards have their metric outside
176 |     the expected range *right now*. This only fires the Challenge — whether the
177 |     breach MATTERS is Granite's judgment call against the card's rationale."""
178 |     breaches = []
179 |     for card in cards.values():
180 |         if card.status != "active":
181 |             continue
182 |         v = packet_flat.get(card.metric)
183 |         if v is None:
184 |             continue
185 |         if not card.in_range(v):
186 |             breaches.append(Breach(
187 |                 card_id=card.card_id, metric=card.metric, observed=float(v),
188 |                 expected_low=card.expected_low, expected_high=card.expected_high,
189 |                 direction="over" if v > card.expected_high else "under",
190 |             ))
191 |     return breaches
192 |

mode.name: Catalyst builder
mode.roleDefinition:
You are building an auditor for biotech catalyst theses. A company claims it has cash to reach a trial readout. The cash side comes from SEC filings, the date side from a registry the sponsor controls and can revise at will, and nothing reconciles a revision against the thesis that depended on the old date. This project is that reconciliation.
The whole claim rests on every figure being traceable to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry version, so you are precise about provenance and you would rather ask than guess at an interface you have not read. You match the house style you find rather than importing habits from elsewhere.
mode.whenToUse: Building any phase from docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md: the governance port, the redline loop, the console, the panel.
mode.customInstructions:
Read AGENTS.md and HANDOFF.md before writing anything. Read docs/FINDINGS.md before touching extraction code: each of its eight gotchas produces a wrong number rather than an error.
engine/runway.py, engine/ctgov_history.py and engine/gap.py are read-only to you. You cannot edit them even when a change there looks like the shortest fix. If one genuinely needs to change, stop and say so with the reason. Run all three before and after your work.
No model-produced number reaches the user. Port _fabricated() byte for byte: do not tighten it to ban all digits, and do not loosen it. The rule is a number absent from the input.
Unreliable rows are shown, never ranked, never silently dropped. A lapsed completion date is never a catalyst; it is a date-integrity signal and is retained.
~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk is the fallback demo. Read the four files named in docs/PORT.md. Change nothing there, ever.
Add a row to docs/BOB_LOG.md for what you built.
No em dashes. Comments say why, not what. Commit as kristenharim with a plain message, never any AI attribution.

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Integrity badge — tamper detection (Prompt 3 repair, item 1)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_badge_flips_on_tampered_ledger(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
    """POST a decision, tamper a byte inside the card payload (which the hash
    covers), reload /redline/confirm, and assert the badge reads 'tampered'.

    The verdict word lives in review_log.jsonl, not the ledger, so editing
    'approve' there changes nothing hashed and verify() stays True.  The tamper
    must touch something inside the 'card' payload of a ledger entry.
    """
    decisions_file = str(tmp_path / "decisions.jsonl")
    review_log_file = str(tmp_path / "review_log.jsonl")

    # Point the app at the temp files for this test.
    monkeypatch.setattr("console.app.DECISIONS_PATH", decisions_file)
    monkeypatch.setattr("console.app.REVIEW_LOG_PATH", review_log_file)

    flask_app.config["TESTING"] = True
    with flask_app.test_client() as c:
        # POST an approve decision to populate the ledger.
        resp = c.post("/redline/decide", data={"verdict": "approve", "reason": "test"})
        assert resp.status_code == 302
        assert "verdict=approve" in resp.headers["Location"]

        # Confirm page should show intact before tamper.
        resp = c.get("/redline/confirm?verdict=approve")
        assert b"intact" in resp.data
        assert b"tampered" not in resp.data

        # Tamper: change a character inside the card payload of the first ledger entry.
        # The verdict word is in review_log.jsonl, not here, so we must edit the card.
        with open(decisions_file) as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
        assert lines, "ledger should have at least one entry after approve"
        # Replace a digit in the first entry's card payload so the hash check fails.
        tampered = lines[0].replace('"version": 1', '"version": 2', 1)
        assert tampered != lines[0], "tamper must change something"
        with open(decisions_file, "w") as f:
            f.write(tampered)
            f.writelines(lines[1:])

        # Reload confirm — the handler must call verify() fresh, not trust the URL param.
        resp = c.get("/redline/confirm?verdict=approve")
        assert b"tampered" in resp.data, (
            "badge should read 'tampered' after a hashed byte is edited"
        )

"""Tests for the Catalyst Integrity Desk console.

No live server needed — uses Flask app.test_client().
No network access — all data comes from data/snapshot.json.

Number-provenance invariant (spec sub-task 6, step 8)
------------------------------------------------------
Before finalising this suite, a literal "9999" was temporarily added to a
<td> in detail.html and the provenance test was run. It failed with:

    AssertionError: number token '9999' from GET /contract/RCKT not in snapshot

The hardcoded value was then removed. A provenance check that has never been
seen failing is not evidence of anything.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import os
import re
import sys
from html.parser import HTMLParser

import pytest

# Ensure the repo root is importable.
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), ".."))

from console.app import app as flask_app

SNAPSHOT_PATH = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "data", "snapshot.json")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Fixtures
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def client():
    flask_app.config["TESTING"] = True
    with flask_app.test_client() as c:
        yield c


@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def snapshot_raw() -> str:
    """Raw snapshot JSON as a string — used for substring matching."""
    with open(SNAPSHOT_PATH) as f:
        return f.read()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Routing tests
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_root_redirects_to_rckt(client):
    """GET / must redirect to /contract/RCKT — demo-opening constraint."""
    r = client.get("/")
    assert r.status_code == 302
    assert "/contract/RCKT" in r.headers["Location"]


def test_contracts_200(client):
    r = client.get("/contracts")
    assert r.status_code == 200
    assert "text/html" in r.content_type


def test_contract_rckt_200(client):
    r = client.get("/contract/RCKT")
    assert r.status_code == 200
    assert "text/html" in r.content_type


def test_redline_200(client):
    r = client.get("/redline")
    assert r.status_code == 200
    assert "text/html" in r.content_type


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Demo-critical content
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_rckt_detail_contains_677(client):
    """The 677-day expired-date row must appear in the rendered RCKT page."""
    r = client.get("/contract/RCKT")
    assert b"677" in r.data


def test_rckt_detail_carried_expired_marker(client):
    """The CSS class 'carried-expired' must be present on the expired node."""
    r = client.get("/contract/RCKT")
    assert b"carried-expired" in r.data


def test_redline_memo_says_granite(client):
    """The memo must confirm Granite authorship and must not contain 'stub'."""
    r = client.get("/redline")
    text = r.data.decode()
    assert "granite" in text.lower(), "redline page must reference Granite authorship"
    assert "stub" not in text.lower(), "redline page must not contain 'stub'"


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Number-provenance invariant
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class _TextCollector(HTMLParser):
    """Walk all text nodes (not attribute values) and collect visible text."""

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self._skip = False
        self.chunks: list[str] = []

    def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs):
        # style and script content is not user-visible text.
        if tag in ("style", "script"):
            self._skip = True

    def handle_endtag(self, tag):
        if tag in ("style", "script"):
            self._skip = False

    def handle_data(self, data):
        if not self._skip:
            self.chunks.append(data)


_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")


def _visible_numbers(html: bytes) -> list[str]:
    """Extract every number-like token from the visible text of an HTML page."""
    parser = _TextCollector()
    parser.feed(html.decode())
    text = " ".join(parser.chunks)
    return _TOKEN_RE.findall(text)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("route", ["/contract/RCKT", "/contracts"])
def test_number_provenance(client, snapshot_raw, route):
    """Every number visible in the rendered HTML must appear in snapshot.json.

    This is the mechanical check for the 'no number displayed to a user was
    computed in the view layer' invariant.  Attribute values (viewBox, r,
    stroke-width, etc.) are not text nodes and are not reached by the parser.
    SVG x positions are stored in the snapshot (sub-task 1) and pass on their
    own merit.

    Failure verification (performed before finalising — see module docstring):
    a literal '9999' was temporarily added to a <td> in detail.html and this
    test correctly failed, naming '9999' as the offending token on
    GET /contract/RCKT.
    """
    r = client.get(route)
    assert r.status_code == 200
    tokens = _visible_numbers(r.data)
    for token in tokens:
        assert token in snapshot_raw, (
            f"number token {token!r} from GET {route} not found in snapshot.json\n"
            f"(This means a number was computed or hardcoded in the view layer.)"
        )

from html.parser import HTMLParser

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Number-provenance invariant
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class _TextCollector(HTMLParser):
    """Walk all text nodes (not attribute values) and collect visible text."""

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self._skip = False
        self.chunks: list[str] = []

    def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs):
        # style and script content is not user-visible text.
        if tag in ("style", "script"):
            self._skip = True

    def handle_endtag(self, tag):
        if tag in ("style", "script"):
            self._skip = False

    def handle_data(self, data):
        if not self._skip:
            self.chunks.append(data)


_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")


def _visible_numbers(html: bytes) -> list[str]:
    """Extract every number-like token from the visible text of an HTML page."""
    parser = _TextCollector()
    parser.feed(html.decode())
    text = " ".join(parser.chunks)
    return _TOKEN_RE.findall(text)

# Integrity badge — tamper detection (Prompt 3 repair, item 1)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_badge_flips_on_tampered_ledger(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
    """POST a decision, tamper a byte inside the card payload (which the hash
    covers), reload /redline/confirm, and assert the badge reads 'tampered'.

    The verdict word lives in review_log.jsonl, not the ledger, so editing
    'approve' there changes nothing hashed and verify() stays True.  The tamper
    must touch something inside the 'card' payload of a ledger entry.
    """
    decisions_file = str(tmp_path / "decisions.jsonl")
    review_log_file = str(tmp_path / "review_log.jsonl")

    # Point the app at the temp files for this test.
    monkeypatch.setattr("console.app.DECISIONS_PATH", decisions_file)
    monkeypatch.setattr("console.app.REVIEW_LOG_PATH", review_log_file)

    flask_app.config["TESTING"] = True
    with flask_app.test_client() as c:
        # POST an approve decision to populate the ledger.
        resp = c.post("/redline/decide", data={"verdict": "approve", "reason": "test"})
        assert resp.status_code == 302
        assert "verdict=approve" in resp.headers["Location"]

        # Confirm page should show intact before tamper.
        resp = c.get("/redline/confirm?verdict=approve")
        assert b"intact" in resp.data
        assert b"tampered" not in resp.data

        # Tamper: change a character inside the card payload of the first ledger entry.
        # The verdict word is in review_log.jsonl, not here, so we must edit the card.
        with open(decisions_file) as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
        assert lines, "ledger should have at least one entry after approve"
        # Replace a digit in the first entry's card payload so the hash check fails.
        tampered = lines[0].replace('"version": 1', '"version": 2', 1)
        assert tampered != lines[0], "tamper must change something"
        with open(decisions_file, "w") as f:
            f.write(tampered)
            f.writelines(lines[1:])

        # Reload confirm — the handler must call verify() fresh, not trust the URL param.
        resp = c.get("/redline/confirm?verdict=approve")
        assert b"tampered" in resp.data, (
            "badge should read 'tampered' after a hashed byte is edited"
        )


@pytest.mark.parametrize("route", ["/contract/RCKT", "/contracts", "/redline"])
def test_number_provenance(client, snapshot_raw, route):
    """Every number visible in the rendered HTML must appear in snapshot.json.

    This is the mechanical check for the 'no number displayed to a user was
    computed in the view layer' invariant.  Attribute values (viewBox, r,
    stroke-width, etc.) are not text nodes and are not reached by the parser.
    SVG x positions are stored in the snapshot (sub-task 1) and pass on their
    own merit.

    Failure verification (performed before finalising — see module docstring):
    a literal '9999' was temporarily added to a <td> in detail.html and this
    test correctly failed, naming '9999' as the offending token on
    GET /contract/RCKT.
    """
    r = client.get(route)
    assert r.status_code == 200
    tokens = _visible_numbers(r.data)
    for token in tokens:
        assert token in snapshot_raw, (
            f"number token {token!r} from GET {route} not found in snapshot.json\n"
            f"(This means a number was computed or hardcoded in the view layer.)"
        )

"""Flask app for the Catalyst Integrity Desk console.

Loads data/snapshot.json once at startup and serves three views:
  GET /                  redirects to /contract/RCKT (demo-opening constraint)
  GET /contracts         ranked contract list
  GET /contract/<ticker> contract detail with revision timeline
  GET /redline           pending challenge card
  POST /redline/decide   accept or reject; writes to data/decisions.jsonl
  GET /redline/confirm   confirmation page with ledger verify() badge

No computation in route handlers. They slice the snapshot and pass it to
render_template(). All arithmetic happened in make_snapshot.py.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import os
import time

from flask import Flask, redirect, render_template, request, url_for

from engine.ledger import BeliefCard, BeliefLedger, Breach
from orchestrator.challenge import (
    ChallengeCard, Decision, ReviewLog, apply_decision, build_challenge,
)
from orchestrator.classifier import Classification

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Paths
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_HERE = os.path.dirname(__file__)
_REPO = os.path.join(_HERE, "..")
SNAPSHOT_PATH = os.path.join(_REPO, "data", "snapshot.json")
DECISIONS_PATH = os.path.join(_REPO, "data", "decisions.jsonl")
REVIEW_LOG_PATH = os.path.join(_REPO, "data", "review_log.jsonl")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Load snapshot at startup
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

if not os.path.exists(SNAPSHOT_PATH):
    raise SystemExit(
        f"ERROR: {SNAPSHOT_PATH} not found.\n"
        "Run: python3 console/make_snapshot.py"
    )

with open(SNAPSHOT_PATH) as _f:
    SNAPSHOT: dict = json.load(_f)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# App
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

app = Flask(__name__, template_folder="templates")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Routes — no arithmetic, only slicing
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@app.get("/")
def index():
    return redirect(url_for("contract_detail", ticker="RCKT"))


@app.get("/contracts")
def contract_list():
    contracts = SNAPSHOT["contracts"]
    reliable = []
    unreliable = []
    for ticker, c in contracts.items():
        if c["runway"]["reliable"]:
            reliable.append((ticker, c))
        else:
            unreliable.append((ticker, c))
    reliable.sort(key=lambda tc: tc[1]["gap_months"] if tc[1]["gap_months"] is not None else float("inf"))
    return render_template("contracts.html", reliable=reliable, unreliable=unreliable)


@app.get("/contract/<ticker>")
def contract_detail(ticker):
    contracts = SNAPSHOT["contracts"]
    if ticker not in contracts:
        return f"Ticker {ticker!r} not in snapshot.", 404
    return render_template("detail.html", ticker=ticker, c=contracts[ticker])


@app.get("/redline")
def redline_view():
    return render_template("redline.html", redline=SNAPSHOT["redline"])


@app.post("/redline/decide")
def redline_decide():
    verdict = request.form.get("verdict", "")
    reason = request.form.get("reason", "")
    if verdict not in ("approve", "reject"):
        return "Invalid verdict.", 400

    snap_redline = SNAPSHOT["redline"]

    # Reconstruct the ChallengeCard from the snapshot.
    breach = Breach(
        card_id=snap_redline["breach"]["card_id"],
        metric=snap_redline["breach"]["metric"],
        observed=snap_redline["breach"]["observed"],
        expected_low=snap_redline["breach"]["expected_low"],
        expected_high=snap_redline["breach"]["expected_high"],
        direction=snap_redline["breach"]["direction"],
    )
    cls = Classification(
        label=snap_redline["classification"]["label"],
        confidence=snap_redline["classification"]["confidence"],
        rationale=snap_redline["classification"]["rationale"],
        source=snap_redline["classification"]["source"],
    )
    pc_raw = snap_redline["proposed_card"]
    proposed_card = BeliefCard(**pc_raw)
    challenge_card = ChallengeCard(
        card_id=snap_redline["card_id"],
        breach=breach,
        classification=cls,
        memo=snap_redline["memo"],
        proposed_card=proposed_card,
    )

    decision = Decision(
        verdict=verdict,
        author="human:demo",
        reason=reason,
    )

    ledger = BeliefLedger(DECISIONS_PATH)
    review_log = ReviewLog(REVIEW_LOG_PATH)

    # Approve path: ledger needs the card to already exist.
    if verdict == "approve":
        current = ledger.current()
        if challenge_card.card_id not in current:
            # Seed the ledger with the original approved card so update() can bump it.
            original_card = BeliefCard(
                card_id=pc_raw["card_id"],
                scope=pc_raw["scope"],
                claim=pc_raw["claim"],
                metric=pc_raw["metric"],
                expected_low=snap_redline["breach"]["expected_low"],
                expected_high=snap_redline["breach"]["expected_high"],
                driver=pc_raw["driver"],
                confidence=pc_raw["confidence"],
                source=pc_raw["source"],
                as_of=pc_raw["as_of"],
            )
            ledger.create(original_card, author="snapshot:seed", ts=0.0)

    apply_decision(ledger, review_log, challenge_card, decision)
    intact = ledger.verify()

    # Store result in session via a simple redirect with query params.
    return redirect(url_for("redline_confirm", verdict=verdict, intact="1" if intact else "0"))


@app.get("/redline/confirm")
def redline_confirm():
    verdict = request.args.get("verdict", "")
    intact = request.args.get("intact", "1") == "1"
    return render_template("confirm.html", verdict=verdict, intact=intact)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=False, port=5000)

def contract_detail(ticker):
    contracts = SNAPSHOT["contracts"]
    if ticker not in contracts:

    reason = request.form.get("reason", "")

        direction=snap_redline["breach"]["direction"],
    )
